terms.definition: The official document or method used by a voter to record their choice.
An election held outside the regular schedule to fill a vacancy caused by death or resignation.
Rules governing how much money candidates can raise and spend during an election.
A person who formally seeks election to a public office.
An alliance of political parties that cooperate to form a government together.
A geographic area whose residents are represented by one elected official.
A structured discussion between candidates where they present and defend their positions.
A system of government where citizens exercise power by voting for their representatives.
All citizens who are eligible to vote in a given election.
A survey conducted as voters leave polling stations to predict the election outcome before official results.
An electoral system where the candidate with the most votes wins, even without a majority.
The right to vote in public elections.
Manipulating the boundaries of electoral districts to favor one party or group.
The current holder of an elected office who is seeking re-election.
A public declaration by a political party outlining their policies and promises if elected.
The formal process of putting forward a candidate to stand in an election.
An independent monitor who watches election proceedings to ensure fairness.
A designated location where voters go to cast their ballots on election day.
An electoral system where seats are allocated based on the percentage of votes each party receives.
The minimum number of members required to be present for a legislative body to conduct official business.
A repeat tallying of votes, usually requested when the margin of victory is very small.
A direct vote by the electorate on a specific policy question rather than choosing a candidate.
The official responsible for conducting the election in a specific constituency and declaring the result.
A voting method where choices are confidential, protecting voters from pressure or retaliation.
The right to vote in political elections.
A rule restricting how many times or how long a person can hold a particular elected office.
The percentage of eligible voters who actually cast a ballot in an election.
The official list of all registered voters eligible to vote in an election.
Official identification required in many countries to prove identity before casting a ballot.
A constituency where the result is uncertain and could be won by more than one party.
